topic: I built a Claude Code plugin that writes entire articles autonomously — here's how it works and what I learned
title: I built a Claude Code plugin that writes entire articles autonomously
subtitle: A skills-based architecture that handles research, outlining, writing, and quality evaluation — and what actually surprised me about the process
summary: Most AI writing tools give you a text box and a 'generate' button. I wanted something different — an agent that does its own research, writes in proper narrative prose instead of bullet-point soup, and keeps iterating until quality gates actually pass. So I built an article-writer plugin for Claude Code using the skills architecture. It supports 9 content types from 10,000-word long-form pieces to reddit posts like this one, and every target is driven by a spec file so nothing is hardcoded. Here's what the architecture looks like and what I learned building it.

editorial_standards.header_style: ultra-casual or no headers, never formal

narrative_arc.hook: The frustration with existing AI writing tools — they produce bullet-heavy, generic content that reads like a corporate memo. What if an AI agent could actually write like a person?
narrative_arc.tension: Building an autonomous writing pipeline is harder than it sounds. Quality control, research depth, prose vs bullets, iteration loops — all need to work together.
narrative_arc.resolution: A skills-based architecture where each phase (PRD, research, outline, write, review, eval) is a separate skill with its own rules, and everything reads from a single spec file.
narrative_arc.transformation: Readers understand how to think about autonomous agent architectures for content — and maybe build their own.

sections.title: Why I started building this

scaffold.opening_hook: Every AI writing tool I tried produced the same thing — listicle-shaped content that reads like someone fed a prompt into ChatGPT and hit publish.
scaffold.central_argument: The problem isn't AI writing itself. It's that most tools optimize for speed over quality, and there's no feedback loop to catch when the output is garbage.
key_questions: Why does most AI-generated content read the same?
What's actually missing from current AI writing workflows?
What would it look like if an AI agent wrote the way a good editor thinks?
example_types: specific example of bad AI output
scaffold.closing_bridge: So I decided to build something that actually iterates on quality instead of just generating and shipping.

research_questions: What are the common criticisms of AI-generated content in 2025-2026?
What do professional writers and editors say about AI writing quality?
What autonomous AI writing tools exist and what are their limitations?

sections.title: How the whole thing fits together

scaffold.opening_hook: The plugin is built on Claude Code's skills architecture — each phase of writing is a separate skill that knows its job and reads its targets from one source of truth.
scaffold.central_argument: Breaking the writing process into discrete skills (PRD, research, outline, write-section, embed-media, review, eval) with a central spec file makes the whole system composable and debuggable.
key_questions: Why separate skills instead of one big prompt?
How does the spec file drive everything downstream?
What makes this different from just chaining prompts together?
scaffold.closing_bridge: The architecture is simple on paper. The interesting part is what happens when you add quality gates.

research_questions: How does Claude Code's skills/plugin architecture work?
What patterns exist for multi-step autonomous agent workflows?
How do other writing agent systems handle orchestration?

sections.title: The parts that actually matter

scaffold.opening_hook: Three things made the biggest difference: content type presets, the eval loop, and the prose-over-bullets philosophy baked into every skill.
scaffold.central_argument: The secret sauce isn't any single feature — it's that quality rules are enforced at every stage, not just checked at the end. And the content type system means you get appropriate structure whether you're writing a 10k deep-dive or a reddit post.
key_questions: How do content types change the writing behavior?
Why does the eval loop matter more than the initial generation?
What does enforcing prose ratio actually do to output quality?
scaffold.closing_bridge: It's not perfect — there are real limitations.

research_questions: How do quality gates in AI agent systems improve output?
What research exists on prose vs bullet-heavy content engagement?
How do content type systems work in other writing platforms?

sections.title: What I'd do differently

scaffold.opening_hook: This thing isn't magic. It still needs a human to check the output, and the research phase can burn through context fast on long articles.
scaffold.central_argument: Autonomous doesn't mean unsupervised. The biggest lesson was that the quality gates are only as good as your spec — garbage in, garbage out still applies even with a feedback loop.
key_questions: Where does human oversight still matter?
scaffold.closing_bridge: If you're building something similar, the repo is open source — feel free to steal the approach.

research_questions: What are known limitations of autonomous AI writing agents?
How are other builders approaching quality control in AI content systems?
What's the state of Claude Code plugin development and the ecosystem?

metadata.target_audience: Developers and technical users interested in Claude Code, AI agents, and autonomous content creation workflows
